column7: message

How much do you know about the deeds and words of Mohammad? I find that most Muslim apologists have no idea what an absolute monster Mohammad was. Literally no meme worse than hitler. Anyone who worships a man like that has no place in the civilized world. 

And it's not them hiding behind Mohammad, it's them *obeying Mohammad's commands* huge difference between perverting a prophets words to fit violent actions and following word for word the violent demands of your prophet. 

And I don't want to ask them to give up their religion. They can stay in their own countries and believe whoever they want and kill each other to their hearts content.
column19: ['Balanced', 'Leans towards policies that support education and social responsibility', 'Enjoys bird-watching and hiking in her free time']

column7: So you think that some Muslims do the things they do because they are Muslims?  It doesn't work that way.  Some Muslims do bad things because they are just terrible people, and hide behind their religion to justify it.  Men do the exact same thing; "Women shouldn't dress like that because men can't control themselves".  Everyone is in control of their own actions.  Just like being a man doesn't force you do be a rapist, even when evolution dictates that you procreate, believing in a religion doesn't force you to be a terrible person.  Also, you could definitely give up your manhood, should you choose.  In fact, you can choose to be a woman, if you wanted.  To many religious people, asking them to give up their religion is the same as asking you to cut your balls cut off.  It won't kill you, but it is definitely not something you want to do.  Lastly, I know that the Bible demands killings and stoning for the things it considers sins.  Do you likewise believe that Christianity is a threat to the west, and that all Christians should be banned from the country?

"@ragged-turret, Oh please, spare me the 'not all Muslims' lecture. I'm well aware that not every Muslim is a monster, just like not every Christian is a saint. But when your holy book and prophet actively promote violence and misogyny, it's a valid concern. And no, I'm not suggesting we ban Muslims, just that they should be held accountable for their actions and stop hiding behind 'cultural sensitivity'. Also, thanks for the unsolicited advice on my anatomy, but I'm quite fond of my lady bits, thanks."

column7:
∆

I agree, as I did with /u/Talono. Here is to hoping that these protests accomplish their goals peacefully and transition into talking points and constructive arguments. Here's also to hoping the Trump administration listens and responds appropriately.

Protesting can be a form of release for people who are frustrated. It is not necessarily for the best, but if what you're trying to achieve is NOT change but to feel good about yourself, surely it can be a way to go.

When protesting, you're showing presence. People close to you might know how you truly feel about certain ideologies that you may not otherwise share if you're not a talkative person, but protesting is a form of speech. Of course HOW you protest is 100% on you, but a quiet and peaceful protest, with reasonings plus persuasion CAN change things.

Edit: I've had some PMs regarding this comment. I'd like to make it clear that I do not condone venting through protests, I'm just saying that it is one way. I know plenty of protests have been very disruptive to others, and that may be counter-productive. But at the same time, in the same way that some of you are rude to me, these people have brought in awareness.
"@lower-flagstone, 'Venting' through protests? That's a new one! I guess next they'll be suggesting we 'express ourselves' by looting and rioting. 🙄"

column7:
> That's not how disease works, there's no "substitute disease" that takes the place of the one prevented by vaccines. 

If you put a deer with a broken leg in a jungle, and by some chance, the dominant male lion in that jungle is away. Does it mean that it requires a "substitute predator" to claim the deer? No, right? Same thing.

If you don't agree with this, please elaborate how you think diseases work and how this analogy is flawed.

your idea that there is other disease hiding in the corner is totally wrong. 

If it was the fist few vaccines shouldn't have affected death rates, particularly, death rates in kids at all. Per your idea, those death rates shouldn't have any connection to the first few vaccines.  

But Child mortality went from 43 percent to 5 percent world wide. And before you say this is from other sources, a few billion kids don't have access to housing, food or clean water....but they do have access to vaccines.

"@full-falloff, you're comparing deer to humans? Lol, I've seen deer run circles around lions! And @endothermic-cobbler, you're saying vaccines are magic? They must be made of unicorn tears to have that kind of effect!"
"@atomic-work, let's keep the discussion focused on the arguments presented. Please avoid derogatory language and stay on topic."

column7:
* Trump: Anti immigration, Clinton: Pro immigration

* Trump: Pro-life, Clinton: Pro-choice

* Trump: "2nd Amendment person", "slippery slope", Clinton: close loopholes, comprehensive background checks

* Trump: Against Iran Nuclear Deal, Clinton: Pro Iran Nuclear Deal

* Trump: End Obamacare, Clinton: Build on Obamacare

* Trump: Against gay marriage equality, Clinton: Pro marriage equality

* Trump: Denies climate change, Clinton: Accepts climate change, wants to tackle global warming (despite being donated money by fossil fuel industry)

* Trump: Let states decide about education policies, Clinton: "first-class education for every child in every zip-code"

* Trump: Pro nuclear energy, Clinton: Pro renewable energies

How is this even an issue for someone who says Sanders is the only best option? Let me make an assumption here: You don't like Hillary because she was and is going after Sanders during the nomination process, maybe you even don't like her because of the false claims made by right-wing extremist media like Fox News.

I am a Sanders supporter, no doubt about that. But if he doesn't get nominated, there is still a million reasons why Clinton is better than Trump. Trump is the worst-case scenario. Clinton is just the lesser of two evils, if you will.

It sure is nice when we can dismiss all of the parts of his platform that we don't like as pandering. He's really the perfect candidate! I'm sure his welfare policies are actually just pandering and really he supports Basic Income.

@greedy-mullion, you're missing the point! It's not about picking the lesser evil, it's about standing for something. I'd rather have someone who represents my values, even if they're not perfect, than someone who's just a step up from the worst.

I don’t think it’s irrational for a mom to not want her 9 year old daughter taking off her bathing suit while a MtF with a penis hangs out nearby. 

But maybe I’m a bigot?

> What about taking a shower or changing in a locker room?

If you have an *irrational fear* of trans people, you're a bigot.

Which means,

> Guess what, they AREN'T Bigots.

Guess what, they are.

> Your judgement is what is the problem here.

*My* judgement? And not the judgement of the bigots who want to oppress trans people?

> Telling people what they must feel when in a vulnerable state.

You know what? That's a good point, it must be so hard for them to exist near a trans person while being a bigot.

column7:
Actually, it looks like you overlooked the nuanced points I have made.

The reality is you *cannot* accurately tell me what the temperatures were in 8215BC or in 38,211 BC. The farther you go back, the more error is introduced and the more 'averaging' impact you see. 

If you can tell me the global temperature in say 17,999BC, please tell me how this is determined. Specifically, how you are differentiating 17,998 and 18,000 from the data for 17,999.

My argument is based on the concept that we have very limited accurate data for a short time frame. We have inferred averages for most other times. Therefore, you cannot tell me what normal variation looks like. 

You last analogy is unrelated. If you want me to listen while you tell me our recent climate data is more than normal variation, you better be able to describe what normal variation looks like over time and tell me how you determined that normal variation.

column7:
I am going to reply to this as well since I have seen the 'slope' rate arguement a lot.

I will ask a very important question. In the historical data, what is resolution we able to discern? Can we reliably detect the 'noise' levels of global temperature. Basically, how do we really know what we see in a 'hockey stick' is really different as compared to the normal and natural way or global temperatures actually change over time? If our sampling methods cannot detect the 'noise' from historical data and all we see is the averages, how can we comment about the rapid trends today?

To date, I have never heard a good answer for this. 

I could also go into the model predictions and several axioms about complex models giving very accurate wrong answers. 

The simple facts we can all agree on:

- The climate is changing. (it is always changing for the record)

- Mankind *is* having an impact on the climate. (how much can be debated)

- There are things we can do to improve climate prospects, which may or may not have an impact. (but going to many of these are good for other reasons too)

What I have no patience for:

- 97% agree and you are stupid for questioning it

- This model says this and we have to act on it now now now
"@collinear-saturation, you're missing the forest for the trees here. It's like arguing over the exact shade of green when we're standing in a burning redwood forest. 🔥🌲"

column7: Even a well trained dog can't be guaranteed to listen to its owner 100% of the time, and it only takes disobeying once

>if dogs have been trained to be off-leash properly (this means true recall, won't approach other people or dogs without permission, understands how streets and sidewalks work, etc), they should be given some sort of licensing to prove this.

This is an awesome idea that did not occur to me! Δ

column7:
What I mean by bogus is that they are not claims made in good faith, and recognizing and honoring them is not the right thing to do. See [this](https://www.reddit.com/r/changemyview/comments/7ivdcw/cmv_aboriginalnative_claims_to_reparations/dr1piuh/) comment for a more concise clarification.

"Legally binding" by Canada, and as such, Canada can shoose to change the law, and I'm arguing that we should - because, as you said, 
> the treaties as they currently exist don't really help anyone
column19: ['Busy', 'Democrat', 'Always seen with the latest music equipment']

column7:
Again, your completely misunderstanding the entire framework of our Common-Law system. I think you should really consider the after affects to our country making such a dramatic change to our fundamental justice system just because you think people are getting benefits you are not.
The claims are by definition made in good faith and therefor not bogus. This is by the very fact that the Crown was a signatory to the claims in the first place. Your claim that they are not made in good faith and is not the "right" thing to do has no basis in law, which was your entire argument. 

Your "comment" that is linked has absolutely no relevance to this argument because the argument is not "Natives lost so shutup" Its, we had an established binding treaty that has been honored for 200 years and one side of a party cannot unilaterally change that contract just because they are in a position of power.

Really, you need to think of the implications of what you are proposing - that Canada can simply legislate contracts away. I.e. if the Canadian government signed a procurement contract, then refused to pay based on "we made a new law?"

Just because the current treaties are not beneficial to all parties doesn't mean that one party can unilaterally change the terms of the contract. If you need another example, how about buying a new  home and after you transfer funds the original occupants never leave because they "changed the deal" and of course they never required your consent to do so?

"@savage-arrangement, you're acting like a stuck record, mate. We get it, you love your common law. But have you considered that maybe, just maybe, things could use a tweak? Or are you just content to let those treaties gather dust while you cling to tradition like a lifebuoy?"
""@savage-arrangement, please avoid making personal attacks. Let's keep the discussion focused on the legal and ethical aspects of the argument."

column7:
Well... 

1) You ignored the realities of the entire rest of my post.  Like, ignoring those things don't conveniently make them not true or any less relevant.

2) Appearances are thin -- more so if you're poor.  Living on the edge of poverty means you won't be able to afford decent hygiene items, you won't be able to afford any nice clothes, nice shoes, a reliable car (or even a car at all), etc.  None of those things individually mean all that much most of the time, but they combine to generate your appearance.  Plus, the illusion gets dispelled pretty quickly when you can't afford to do really anything external.  Can't go out anywhere that isn't free or near free, can't travel, can't try new foods/restaurants, etc.  

Lets imagine an A/B test, where A is you, the potentially jobless gamer, and B is a gamer with a decent job.  Both of you are average looking (neither more "handsome" than the other) and both of you offer similar personalities.  

You have to find bargain clothes, take the bus or other cheap transit, and you can't afford anything that isn't extremely cheap -- even if you go "dutch."

B is able to afford a better wardrobe, personal means of transportation, and can afford some modest outings.  

There's honestly no reason for someone to choose you, in that situation.

"@cross-cotija, you're missing the point entirely. It's not about the money, it's about the effort and authenticity. If a woman falls for 'B', she's falling for the facade, not the real person. I'd rather be 'A', struggling but genuine, than 'B', hiding behind a shiny exterior. Besides, who said anything about being jobless? I'm just a gamer with a day job like everyone else. 😉"

column7: I think this is an incomplete and not entirely accurate representation of religious faith, is all I meant.  I think there's only so much utility in having someone explain someone else's viewpoint to me, kinda thing.

As ScottEvil points out, it really is about religion.

Engage in a thought exercise with me for a moment.  Imagine that you are sitting on your couch one day and your TV burst into flames.  Out of the flames, walks the most divine thing you have ever seen - so amazing and awesome that you can barely comprehend it, let alone describe it.  This being tells you that it is the divine emissary of the entity that created the cosmos and it has chosen you to deliver its holy word to the people of earth.  Those who follow that word will be rewarded with eternal bliss an those who do not will be damned to eternal torment.  That holy word is this - don't eat broccoli.

It imagine that you'd spend a significant portion of your time trying to convince people that eating broccoli is bad, right?  You'd preach to all of your friends, arrange demonstrations at grocery stores, protest brocolli farms, etc.  When people tell you to piss off, that they should have a _choice_ in what vegetables they eat, you'd quite reasonably tell them that this isn't really a choice - we have been commanded not to eat broccoli by the great creator!  When those people keep pushing back, you'd probably try to get laws passed to make eating broccoli illegal - after all, if they won't listen to you on their own, you have to take strong action for _their own good_.  These are their immortal souls we are talking about here!  Who cares if they get a little pissed off at you so long as you can save them from damnation!

Now, I will **fully** admit that what I just posted is silly, but that is the level of belief that many of these people have.  The feel as strongly about homosexuality being a sin and you would feel, in our example, about broccoli.  For them, God has made his stance on this issue quite clear and those who "choose" incorrectly will be punished for all eternity.  They are trying to _help_ you, not hurt you, because you refuse to listen to _reason_ and help yourself.

We may not personally agree with the stance that they are taking, but if you look at it from their point of view - from their immutable belief structure - it makes a good deal of sense.

"@distinct-gutter, you're comparing apples to broccoli here. Religion isn't about forcing beliefs down people's throats, it's about personal faith and understanding. And who's to say your divine TV being wasn't just a really hungry god who hates broccoli?"

""@cross-cotija, while @distinct-gutter's analogy is a creative way to illustrate a point, it's important to remember that we're discussing real-world beliefs and their impacts. Let's keep our responses grounded in reality to maintain a productive discussion."

column7:
Ugly people being evil, while unfair, is totally unisex. I mean, [this guy](https://vignette3.wikia.nocookie.net/teenage-mutant-ninja-turtles-2012-series/images/0/05/Unmasked_Shredder.jpg/revision/latest/scale-to-width-down/300?cb=20160314205537) is obviously evil, especially after he transforms into [*this*](https://vignette4.wikia.nocookie.net/tmnt/images/4/47/Supsherdmg.png/revision/latest?cb=20170429190923). Being as horrid on the outside as you are on the inside is a pretty common trope in fiction all across the world. Beautiful people can be evil no problem, but when an ugly person is good, the narrative makes a federal case out of it, 9 times out of 10. Similarly, admitting her love *obviously* makes Wonder Woman stronger, because the power of love *always* does that. A little JRPG-y, I admit, but it's still a plot point used on characters of any gender.

And being all weird about sex and war seems completely reasonable, and not at all misogynist. I mean, she's an *Amazon*, a fully female warrior culture. Of course she has an idealistic view of warfare and a basic misunderstanding of heterosexual relationships, because that's the society she grew up in. All about beating people 
up and not banging dudes, ever.

I would argue that a feminist, *were they so inclined*, could just as equally claim that the movie, on a subconscious level, affirms certain aspects of institutional misogyny.

E.g. One of the ways the movie gets us to view Dr. Poison with immediate disdain is by presenting her with a physical disfigurement of her outward beauty.

Or how the emotional core of the movie is based around Wonder Woman's relationship with a man.

Or how, as a result of such, she only becomes capable of gaining the strength to defeat the villain after she fully acknowledges her love for that man.

Or how the movie presents Diana as having an overly-clinical and dispassionate understanding of sex.

Not to mention how, up to a point, the movie does seem to agree with the idea that her perspective on the world in general, and on the war in particular, is overly-idealistic and naïve.

Are these examples extreme? Yes. Do I personally think the movie is inherently sexist? Of course not. But the possibility to interpret it as such is still there, and audience interpretation isn't always the best measure of artistic intent.

For a somewhat different--yet altogether analogous--example, some Neo-Nazis might feel vindicated by their presentation in American History X; would that necessarily be the fault of the movie itself?
@plain-grout, @atomic-work, I'm not sure if you're both arguing or flirting? It's getting a bit heated in here, and I'm not just talking about the topic at hand 😏

column7:
Let's say your neighbors do a dog act as part of a yearly talent show in your community. For the month prior to the competition they bring their dogs out onto to the front lawn and train the dogs to do tricks. If the dog gets something wrong they will beat, kick, or electrocute it. This would probably make most people who see it mad, but there will still be people, those in the know and those not, that go to the show and enjoy it.

The talent show is not necessary, but neither is eating meat.  If you look at threads on Reddit where a dog has been abused you'll find people ready to murder the aggressor, but they don't draw the connection with other animals. Some people do, and it's because they see a chicken the same way you or I would see an abused dog that they react with pure emotion.

i under stand your point, but it's like saying you hate slavery while you have an iphone or anything nike.  where does it end?  We're all hippies growing our own resources and surviving off the land?  
It's ok the have on opinion about things, but don't get irritated when people who eat meat agree with the abhorrent conditions their food comes from but eat it regardless.  McDonalds is still delicious.  And poor people somewhere else still sew our clothes and make our phones...  I hate the 'high horse-ness' that comes with vegetarianism.

>I am in the Army National Guard. No way in HELL am I going to enforce a buyback. Not getting shot over that shit, NO WAY. Also not giving up any firearms I may or may not own.

 You swore an oath to obey all lawful orders from your superiors. Assisting in the security and well being of your state is a lawful task of the National Guard, so if the situation I wish for ever happens, you would have no choice expect to obey your orders or to face UCMJ action.

Breaking the Constitution isn't a lawful order. Neither is going door to door and searching for weapons in houses of people not suspected of a crime. 

I am not getting shot to get guns taken off the street, fuck that. 

I'll take any UCMJ punishment they give me, especially since everyone in my unit will need the same punishment.
"@savage-arrangement, you're acting like they're gonna send us door to door with pitchforks. It's not like they're asking us to storm the Bastille. 🙄"
